data.title: Byrna Technologies: Q3 Sent Strong Growth Signals
data.description:
Byrna Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:BYRN) reported strong fiscal Q3 results from the June-August period. The less-lethal self defense launcher company continues to show strong momentum, driven by e-commerce and wholesale retail growth. The stock reacted very strongly to Byrna’s strong growth signals in the report, ending the day up by 21%.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my initial August article, titled “Byrna Technologies: Aiming To Capture A Vast Market, But Success Isn't A Given”. The stock has since returned 42%, well above the S&P 500’s 4% return.

Byrna’s Q3 Sent Strong Growth Signals

Byrna’s Q3 report was strong. Revenues came in at $28.2 million, up by 35% year-on-year through very fast wholesale retail expansion. Growth in Byrna’s own retail channel, international sales, and direct-to-consumer ecommerce also came in at a good level, as the growth story is progressing well. The revenue momentum itself isn’t a reason for Byrna’s stock surge - the revenue figure was already known by the market, as Byrna previously already gave its preliminary Q3 revenues in early September. The topline figure also didn’t show much cause for celebration on a sequential basis, even showing a slight decrease.

Rather, the report sent strong signals of Byrna’s upcoming growth, setting up higher future expectations for Q4 and FY2026. The company previously anticipated 25-40% growth but now guides more enthusiastic 35-40% revenue growth for FY2025 – Q4 revenues are now implied at $35.0 million at the guidance mid-point.

Most importantly, in my opinion, Byrna’s report and earnings call remarks showed very strong signs of upcoming ecommerce growth. Byrna’s own web traffic has improved to average daily web sessions of 58 thousand in September, up from just 33 thousand prior to new advertising campaigns. The momentum has sustained with several days of 70 thousand daily web sessions in October. The Amazon ecommerce channel has followed with similar momentum.

While web growth came in at just 8% in Q3 with web revenues of $16.3 million, improving web conversion rates are seen to drive much faster growth in Q4 and beyond - ecommerce revenue momentum is clearly only starting to build. Byrna’s Semrush statistics also clearly show a continued surge in traffic. Byrna’s “We Don’t Sell Bananas” AI ad has clearly succeeded, and the company is planning ambitious AI and influencer advertising campaigns ahead.

Byrna is now in over 1000 retail locations over the U.S., driving most of the revenue momentum seen in Q3. Yet, as Byrna doesn’t want to become overly saturated, CFO Laurilee Kearnes told in the earnings call that Byrna will probably mostly be holding where Byrna is on its current retail footprint – Byrna is becoming more selective about additional retail partnerships. The revenue stream may not show as rapid growth going forward after the wholesale retail channel has already expanded very quickly.

Adding more SKUs to existing stores and improving brand visibility should still continue to drive growth in retail; Byrna noted significant opportunities within the existing store footprint. Underlining the notation, Sportsman’s Warehouse (SPWH) noted that the retailer has a lot of upside with Byrna and other less-lethal self-defense options in its latest earnings call.

Profitability Is Following Byrna’s Growth

The second main positive in the Q3 report was Byrna’s profitability – adjusted EBITDA came in at $3.7 million, up by 91.5% year-on-year at an already strong 13.2% margin. The adjusted EPS of $0.12 beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.07.

The gross margin even temporarily declined due to a larger mix of dealer sales and startup costs related to the Compact Launcher, but operating leverage still provided a platform for significant margin gains during the quarter. Byrna has already proven its business’ profitability despite still growing rapidly. Further growth should enable more margin expansion through operating leverage in production and other functions.

BYRN’s Valuation Doesn’t Provide Base Scenario Upside

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Byrna. I now estimate the company to reach $612.5 million in FY2034 revenues, 3.5% of Byrna’s estimate for the company’s addressable market. I previously assumed a 3.0% market share. The estimates now reflect a rapid FY2024-FY2034 CAGR of 21.7%.

I still expect Byrna to reach a 15% EBIT margin, but now by FY2028 already. I still factor in a 15 exit EV/EBIT multiple, and that Byrna converts after-tax earnings relatively well into free cash flow.

The estimates put Byrna’s fair value at $29.1, near the stock price at the time of writing. While Byrna’s momentum stands strong, much is already priced in – I believe that investors should likely wait for a better entry point. A 3.5% market share of Byrna’s very rough $17.5 billion TAM estimate is already priced in.

The fair value estimate is up from $24.7 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 11.90%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t assume Byrna to draw debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.11% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate quite high at 1.60. With a liquidity premium of 0.4%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 11.90%.

Takeaway

Byrna’s growth story stands strong as e-commerce momentum is picking up and retail expansion has progressed well. Profitability has followed growing revenues. Yet, as the valuation already prices in significant growth, I believe that investors are likely better off waiting for a better entry point. I remain neutral at a Hold rating for BYRN stock.

data.title: Did Gold And Silver Just Peak? (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

After hitting one new high after another, gold, silver, and mining stocks took a breather on Thursday. Gold slipped 1.62% to close at $3,976.24, while silver managed to finish up 1.05% at $49.39, though still well below its earlier intraday high of $51.24.

None of this should come as a surprise or cause for alarm. No asset moves straight up forever. In fact, in my recent writings, I have repeatedly noted that, despite my strong bullish outlook for the broader precious metals bull market, gold and silver have become overheated in the short term.

A shallow pullback or period of sideways consolidation here would be both natural and healthy, allowing them to cool off, reset their overbought conditions, and build the base needed for even greater gains ahead.

On October 3, I said that silver was likely to pause just below its critical $50 psychological level. Rather than crashing, I expected a healthy, shallow pullback or brief consolidation that would allow it to gather strength before ultimately breaking through the $50 level that capped its rallies in 1980 and 2011.

Here’s a relevant excerpt from that piece:

Now I want to say that I adamantly believe silver is going to surpass $50 and go much higher from there in the near future. However, there is something I want to point out. Because silver has surged so strongly and so quickly, it is a bit extended in the short term, which makes it likely that it will consolidate or pause before eventually breaking through $50.

Evidence of this can be seen in the Relative Strength Index (RSI) momentum indicator, shown in the chart below, which provides overbought or oversold readings for assets. I respect what it indicates.

That being said, unlike many amateur investors and analysts, I am adamant that an overbought reading like the current one in silver is not an automatic signal to sell or an indication that the bull market is over.

There is a very high likelihood that silver will experience a shallow pullback or move sideways for a time to work off its overbought condition. This would conserve energy for the next leg higher, when silver smashes through the $50 ceiling.

In that piece, I showed the long-term chart of silver and explained the importance of the $50 level, which capped the rallies in both 1980 and 2011 and led to sharp declines afterward.

Once that level came back into play recently, I pointed out that it would be critical to watch how silver reacts there and that a brief pause or hiccup was likely before it ultimately breaks through (and I expect it to break through this time around).

Now let’s take a look at the daily chart to see how silver behaved today at that critical $50 resistance level. Early in the New York session, it made a sharp attempt to test that level but failed to close above it and then pulled back.

I’m not at all surprised by this since it’s exactly the scenario I was expecting. Everything is unfolding according to the playbook I’ve been following, which is reassuring.

I always prefer when the market behaves as I had anticipated rather than catching me off guard because it reaffirms that I have a solid understanding of the mechanics of the market.

Next, let’s look at the intraday chart so you can see how silver almost exactly touched the $50 level before failing there and selling off about $3 an ounce over the next few hours. That is a textbook example of how key technical levels work and why everyone should pay attention to them.

Those who focus only on fundamentals are living in an ivory tower and are completely out of touch with how the markets work in the real world. Without knowing and following the technicals, it is like flying blind.

Next, I want to show why silver is likely to experience a healthy, shallow pullback or a period of sideways consolidation to work off its overbought condition.

The first chart compares how stretched silver has recently become relative to its 200-day moving average. As you can see, the last few times this occurred, pullbacks or periods of consolidation followed.

However, I do not expect a strong pullback this time because we are still in the early stages of a powerful precious metals bull market, and it is normal to see pauses along the way.

While a brief consolidation may occur, I am not worried at all because silver remains in a confirmed uptrend, and the long-term trend is not at risk. This only refers to short-term market behavior.

Further confirmation that silver has become a bit overheated in the short term can be seen in the Relative Strength Index (RSI), a widely used momentum oscillator that helps identify whether an asset is overbought, oversold, or neutral.

On silver’s daily chart, the RSI recently entered overbought territory. As a reminder, an overbought condition can be worked off through a period of sideways movement; a sharp pullback is not required for that to happen.

Next, let’s look at gold. Just a couple of days ago, everyone was celebrating gold’s move above the critical $4,000 psychological resistance level. But with today’s pullback, it slipped back below that level in both COMEX futures and spot, which makes me pause and think, as it indicates a rejection of that key level.

This increases the likelihood of a modest pullback or a period of sideways consolidation, as gold may have temporarily run out of momentum to push higher in the short term.

That is perfectly normal, healthy, and not at all surprising. It is much like a sprinter who has just run hard and needs a moment to catch their breath.

Like silver, gold became overbought during its powerful surge since late August and has grown extended relative to its 200-day moving average. This indicates that it needs a brief pause to catch its breath before it can resume its upward sprint.

Gold’s Relative Strength Index (RSI) is sending the same message as silver’s, indicating that it is overbought in the short term.

Anyway, to summarize, there is a high likelihood that after their red-hot performances, gold, silver, and mining stocks are due for a healthy short-term pause to catch their breath. I do not expect it to last very long, nor do I believe it will do any damage to the underlying long-term secular bull market.

How am I playing this? Am I dumping my precious metals holdings in a panic? Absolutely not. That could not be further from the truth. I am staying put, still aligned with the uptrend, and doing nothing different.

In fact, yesterday's pullback made me happy because I do not want to see precious metals overheat. I actually welcome a cooldown period because it gives me the opportunity to accumulate more. As an entrepreneur with a rapidly growing income, I want to keep channeling that income into additional precious metals before they take off for the moon.

I do not think many investors are truly ready for the precious metals train to leave the station for good, or for what that would mean for the global economy and world affairs.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Carter's: Oversold With Potential Catalysts
data.description:
Shares of Carter’s (NYSE:CRI) have been a very poor performer over the past year, losing half of their value as the company has struggled with a muted consumer spending environment. While the economy, as a whole, has weathered the significant rise in tariff rates rather well thus far, Carter’s is an example of how tariff policy is having a meaningful impact on some companies and sectors, which are heavily reliant on imported goods with limited pricing power. While the business has faced significant challenges, the sharp decline in the stock may make Carter’s interesting, especially given recent developments.

The baby category is challenged

Carter’s is a leading baby apparel retailer with over 800 locations. The US accounts for about 85% of the business. CRI primarily operates out of its own stores, though it also has a wholesale channel through department stores. This segment has been challenged, as those retailers very closely manage their inventory after struggling with excess inventory for two years. While current management is rightly focused on regaining market share, I see category growth as limited.

Carter’s business faces a simple demographic challenge: we are having fewer babies. It has been well documented that the US, and the developed world as a whole, is seeing its birth rate steadily decline. Births are down about 15% from ~20 years ago. They have plateaued in recent years at the 3.6-3.7 million level. Thus far in 2025, we are on track for ~3.7 million births, comparable to recent years. All else equal, a lower birth rate decreases demand for baby apparel.

As of now, I see no evidence or reason to believe that there will be a reversal of this trend, and this demographic reality simply limits how much the baby category can grow. I also believe that baby-focused retailers are more exposed to demographic challenges than retailers in general. Over time, the US has been able to grow its population as immigration flows offset fewer births. However, while bringing in a 22-year old on a visa can keep the adult population flat and support demand for adult apparel, baby apparel demand does not get a benefit from immigration to the same degree.

Tariffs are squeezing margins

At the same time, tariffs have exacerbated challenges, with recent financials being very weak. In the company’s second quarter, reported back on July 25th, Carter’s earned $0.17, which was $0.20 below expectations even as revenue grew 4% to $585 million. Operating margins shrunk 500 bps to just 2%, even as baby sales were up 10% in Q2, and the company's focus on regaining lost market share in the baby apparel category is showing some progress. Margins are facing a massive squeeze.

As you can see below, Carter is a major importer with significant exposure to Vietnam and Cambodia, leaving it with 20+% tariff costs, while it also has some exposure to China. Management expects about $125-150 million in tariff costs. The H2 impact on pre-tax income will be about $35 million, as it still has some inventory to sell from when tariffs were temporarily 10%.

Management expects to offset these tariff costs in 2026 and beyond, given pricing action, wholesale price cuts, and moving production to lower-tariff countries. Roughly speaking, CRI would need to raise prices by ~4-5% across all products to fully offset tariff costs. I am skeptical of the ability to do that without also causing an impact on volumes, and I expect structural tariff costs of at least $50 million per year.

Frankly, there is limited evidence that CRI can raise prices to this extent. Last quarter, units were up 6%, with pricing & mix a 2% headwind. CRI has been cutting prices to support demand; switching to price increases is likely to be difficult. Because of these pricing actions, margins face significant pressure. While sales were up $21 million from last year, gross profit actually fell by $1 million as margins compressed by 200 bps. At the same time, SG&A grew by 10% to $273 million, causing 290 bps of margin compression. I expect to see increased focus on managing SG&A as a way to offset tariff costs.

This pressure on margins is likely to intensify as CRI begins facing higher-cost inventory. Inventories are up 3% from last year to $619 million. This is in line with sales growth. Notably, the number of units in inventory is down 1%, but the cost is up 4%, primarily due to tariffs. With the cost per unit rising but CRI’s average selling price falling, the margin crunch is likely going to get worse.

Carter's adds a poison pill

While results have been weak, corporate intrigue has risen. Given the weakness in the share price, in late September, Carter’s announced a “temporary stockholder rights plan,” which is commonly known as a poison pill. This maneuver essentially limits the ability of any investor to take more than a 20% stake in CRI. If an investor buys more than 15-20%, CRI will sell shares at a steep discount to all other shareholders to dilute that investor back below 15%. This action occurred after a relatively small hedge fund with $1.3 billion in assets, Roseman Wagner Wealth Management, acquired a ~17% stake in the company.

This action functionally prohibits RWWM from adding to its position, and thus far, there has been no communication between CRI and RWWM. As such, RWWM’s intentions are unknown. It may be seeking to just be a passive investor. It could be tactically investing for a short squeeze (~11% of CRI shares are short). Conversely, it could be planning a proxy fight or hostile bid. The poison pill effectively puts the situation at a standstill until we see what RWWM does next.

I generally look at poison pills negatively. They clearly, in the short term, protect the status quo, which oftentimes protects management more than shareholders. While labelled a shareholder rights plan, a poison pill functionally blocks existing shareholders from selling their shares to a potential buyer of stock. In that sense, poison pills can infringe upon a shareholder’s right. I would like to see this action lead to some publicly documented interactions with RWWM, but for now, we wait to see what the next action will be. I do not believe CRI can keep the poison pill permanently without facing broader shareholder pressure.

Shares are attractive given upside optionality

CRI does have financial flexibility to support shareholders beyond its 3.4% dividend yield. The company has $338 million of cash against $500 million of debt and $626 million of operating leases. Due to buybacks last year, its share count was down 1% from last year. With outside pressure, there could be some renewed repurchase activity. So far this year, it has burnt cash with -$35 million of free cash flow, primarily due to working capital builds. This is about $100 million worse than a year ago, and I expect H2 free cash flow to recover significantly.

Even assuming ~3% of structural margin compression from tariffs, CRI should be a ~6% margin business as it recoups some tariff costs with about $80-100 million in free cash flow. That gives shares an 8-9% free cash flow, which is generally around the fair value I see for apparel-oriented retailers. That said, I do see more upside than downside catalysts, given its cash position, high short interest, and RWWM buying.

I see shares being able to recover into the $30-33 range. CRI has challenged fundamentals, but the stock is pretty fully reflecting this, and there are potential upside catalysts if RWWM launches a proxy fight to remove the poison pill or CRI increases buybacks to alleviate shareholder pressure. As such, I see shares as a “buy” and view the stock as due to bounce despite ongoing challenges.

data.title: Energy Transfer: Powering The AI Boom And Paying An 8% Yield
data.description:
Introduction

While investors see the market skyrocket and debate on whether we are in the AI bubble or not, chasing the next growth story is becoming an imperative. This implies we are leaning toward tomorrow's cash flows, giving up today's profits. But, in this environment, why not take some time to place ourselves in the shoes of those investors who need income today? And what if right there before our eyes lies an opportunity with a high single-digit yield, backed by steady and growing free cash flow generation coming from one of the most strategic assets in North America?

There is no need to create further suspense. You know from the title that we are going to talk about Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET). The midstream giant has truly moved from coping to survive to an efficient capital-returning machine. Its leverage has been normalized, while export volumes, as we will see, are rising. As a result, I see an interesting setup that can combine cyclical upside with income stability and even growth. Since we are talking about an energy infrastructure stock, many would probably agree that this is a rare combination. But let's walk through this thesis one step at a time.

Energy Transfer: Business Model

The company is somewhat adjacent to another big midstream player that I covered in the past: Kinder Morgan (KMI). In some cases, they are even partners (in Florida, for example). We saw how Kinder Morgan plays a pivotal role in U.S. LNG exports through its ownership of strategic pipelines that move gas from the largest basins in North America to the port terminals in the Gulf. Well, Energy Transfer is part of the same strategic midstream infrastructure that transports natural gas from the basins of origin to storage facilities and export terminals. It owns approximately 140k miles of pipelines and makes money by charging fees for moving and transporting natural gas and crude oil. Energy Transfer's revenues are therefore recurring because they come from long-term contracts. Now, being a regulated business, ET can't just raise prices at will, but, at the end of the day, it reports an EBITDA margin of 18.3% and a net income margin just below 6%. What really matters for ET is volumes rather than pricing power. This also has a partial advantage. If commodity prices go down, its fee structure remains almost perfectly intact. However, commodity prices sometimes move south because of lower demand. In this case, ET suffers a hit. We can therefore think of Energy Transfer as a volume-levered business.

As we see below, 90% of its revenues come from fees, while its revenue breakdown by category shows 5 main revenue contributors: midstream natural gas (19%), NGL and refined products (27%), crude oil (20%), intra and interstate (21%). The affiliate investment in Sunoco, the controlled subsidiary USA Compression Partners, and other smaller operations makes up 13% of the company's revenues.

Just to be clear, we should not mix natural gas and NGLs. The first one is mostly methane, which is already a gas at the point of extraction. NGLs are natural gas liquids such as ethane, propane, and butane that become liquid when they are cooled and pressurized. They are extracted from the gas streams and are then sold separately. They have different uses; natural gas is used for power generation and is burned. NGLs have many different uses. Ethane, for example, is used in plastics. Butanes are used for gasoline blending and as refrigerants.

The Macro Context

Before we look at market dynamics, we need to spend two words on the Permian Basin. Most of us know it, especially if we are interested in ET. But there might always be someone who needs a quick overview. In this case, I suggest consulting the RRC webpage, where everything is clearly explained. We should just keep in mind that it is a basin located between West Texas and Southeastern New Mexico. You can see how this is the main area of interest of ET. This basin is the most important oil-and-gas field in the U.S., made up of two main subbasins called Delaware and Midland, separated by the Central Platform, which is a sort of bridge between the two, as shown below. In the reports of the EIA, you will find a lot of interesting stuff on the geologic sections of the basins that allow for drilling oil and gas at different levels. You could think of the rock basin as a layered cake. In each layer, organic sediments were laid that became gas and oil. This means that when you drill, you are actually drilling through more wells at the same time. The basin is huge, and it currently contributes to 40% of U.S. oil production and 15% of U.S. gas.

All of this matters to ET because it guarantees strong multi-commodity throughput.

Let's now turn to some key market indicators. Right now, oil prices are moving down. The Brent spot price was $81 per barrel in 2024 and is now forecasted by the EIA to be $52 next year. At the same time, U.S. crude oil production has been revised upwards both for 2025 and 2026 to 13.5M barrels per day. Natural gas prices are rising and are expected to reach $3.90 per million British thermal units next year, and U.S. LNG gross exports will be 33% higher next year versus 2024 levels. In any case, the new forecast is a 9.3% reduction versus the previous forecast of $4.30. Even though renewables are the second source of electricity generation, natural gas still has the lion's share in the U.S. and in many other places.

The short-term Energy outlook that was released a few days ago also said that oil inventories should rise through 2026, thanks to production growth led by non-OPEC+ countries, with the U.S. reaching record highs of 13.64M barrels per day in July 2025.

At the same time, many projections agree that power demand will grow due to the growing number of data centers used for AI and crypto, as well as the transition to electrification requiring more and more energy.

As we said above, dropping oil prices are not a major concern. There is a 10% share of the company's revenues that is linked to oil prices, but it is a small portion with a minor impact on the company's EBITDA. In particular, this time prices are not falling because of lower demand, but because volumes are rising. However, in inventories swell, we could see slower production activity in 2026, and this could impact Energy Transfer's results. After all, when prices are high, producers are incentivized to drill. But, right now, there are also geopolitical reasons that are making the U.S. drill at record high levels.

At the same time, strong gas and LNG volumes are part of a macroeconomic trend that is probably here to stay for some time. As a result, even in the case oil weakens during 2026, Energy Transfer stands to benefit from gas demand. As of its latest 10-Q, we see that interstate natural gas volumes increased 11%, midstream reported a 10% volume increase, and crude oil volumes were up 9%. So, right now, volumes are solid and growing, and this means further cash flow generation for Energy Transfer.

ET's Financials

I think the macroeconomic picture is clear. But now we have to turn to Energy Transfer itself and see its financials. First and foremost, with midstream companies, we have to be careful with their leverage, which can cause issues during an economic crisis. Well, Energy Transfer has sensibly deleveraged its balance sheet, moving from a Debt/EBITDA ratio of above 7 in 2016 to a more prudent 4x. The debt-to-total capitalization is around 52%, considering that the company's LT debt is around $60.7B and its market cap is $57B. This makes sense since ET has a large asset base (75% of its total assets are in PP&E). Since cash flows are somewhat secured for long times, a 50% leverage is quite acceptable. Secondly, liquidity is fine, with the company having access to a $5B revolver. You will see that the company only holds $242M in cash. But this makes sense too because the balance sheet and the credit profile are built around throughput dynamics that generate consistent cash flows. If the company were more reliant on oil and gas prices, it would, of course, need to insulate its balance sheet and increase its cash position.

Looking at its cash flow, we see that the CFO at the end of Q1 was almost $3B, with capex of $1.22B, which means that the FCF was $1.7B. In Q2, the CFO was $2.8B, with capex of $1.7B, which confirms that the FY outlook will be around $5B.

When we look at the income statement, we see that ET's quarterly revenues declined by almost $1.5B. However, the cost of products sold decreased by more than $1.6B. Now, ET usually passes through NGL and crude costs, so we will see its top line and its COGS move up and down depending on commodity prices. As a result, the company's operating profit held and was actually slightly higher YoY, to a 12% margin, even if D&A increased 14% YoY because of the assets that ET built and that are now in service (for example, Lone Star expansions). SG&A, however, was down 23%, showing cost discipline. Higher investments often mean higher interest expense, and this is the case. But with rates expected to move down, we could see it compress as we move forward.

ET's Valuation

ET is an MLP; as such, we are not going to talk about shares but units. It is also one of the reasons, if not the main one, that explains why ET trades at cheaper multiples compared to Kinder Morgan (KMI). In fact, Energy Transfer is a "pass-through" company that doesn't pay any income tax by giving its investors a more complex ownership stake, tax-wise speaking. In other words, while KMI pays taxes itself, ET doesn't, and each unit holder has to file a K-1 form where their share of the net income is reported. This also explains why ET's yield is much higher than Kinder Morgan's. Because of their complex structure, MLPs usually trade at a discount and have lower liquidity because some funds can't invest in them, and many foreign investors also find it quite complicated to deal with K-1 complexity. This explains why Energy Transfer trades at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 8 while Kinder Morgan's is 12. However, ET's higher returns have contributed over extended time frames (5 and 10 years) to beat Kinder Morgan's total returns. The same is not true when we zoom in and look at shorter time frames. This suggests that Energy Transfer is usually a good deal for income-seeking investors when it is purchased during an energy crisis or poor sentiment, because that is when its yield sensibly increases and leads to above-average returns.

I expect Energy Transfer's future cash flow to grow above $11B in 2026, and this could boost the cash returns to the unitholders. This means that we are paying an 8x multiple while we could have mid-teens returns on equity thanks to the volume growth we expect in 2026. In the past decade, Energy Transfer's cash from operations has roughly tripled from $3.4B to above $11B. In the meantime, CapEx has moderated, stabilizing around $4-5B. The average pattern in the last few years is FCF between $5B and $7B. In the meantime, the stock has traded down. But the key drivers for the next few years could lead us to expect 5% annual growth of ET's CFO. With capex being managed around $5B, we can forecast $7B in FCF by the end of next year, and $8B by the end of 2027. This means we could expect a yield of 10%, which makes Energy Transfer even more attractive.

Since the stock moved down in recent months, I see it currently as a compelling opportunity for income-oriented investors who also want to benefit from favorable macroeconomic energy trends that are unlikely to be wiped away over the next few years.

data.title: October 2025 Trading Outlook: Fiscal Flows, Bank Credit, And Fed Policy Implications
data.description:
Introduction

This report applies the sectoral balance framework to assess how fiscal and monetary dynamics are shaping risk asset performance. With inflation cooling, unemployment rising, and the Federal Reserve signaling a shift in policy, the interplay between fiscal expansion and monetary restraint is increasingly consequential.

Sectoral Balances Overview

The following table tracks monthly changes in sectoral balances, velocity, and acceleration—metrics that help forecast market direction.

Recent data show that September 2025 was a seasonally weak month for fiscal flows, resulting in a net extraction of over $160 billion from the private sector, primarily due to federal government actions. This was partially offset by $75 billion in credit creation from the banking sector, with most credit categories showing growth during the month.

October Fiscal Flow Breakdown

September is seasonally a bad month for fiscal flows, and this September has been no exception, and the result is a net extraction of $160+ billion from the private sector, and this is negative for risk asset prices.

Going into detail: The $160+ billion private sector funds deficit came from a $161 billion extraction of funds from the federal government.

This extraction was offset partially by a healthy $75+ billion of credit creation by the banking sector.

The chart below shows that most types of credit creation grew during the month.

External Sector Impact

The external sector is projected to extract about $75 billion from the domestic private sector in exchange for imported goods and services. While recent trade policies have reduced the trade deficit, the net effect remains a drag on domestic liquidity.

Market Implications

The chart below shows the sectoral balance data plotted in nominal terms. The calculation is federal government spending, or G plus the external sector (X and usually a negative factor). The result is how much money enters or leaves the private domestic sector, or P, an accounting identity true by definition.

Last month the green fiscal flow line was in positive territory, meaning the SPX would most likely finish the month higher than it began, and this was what happened.

This month going forward, the prognosis is the opposite. The green private domestic sector balance line is in negative territory and predicting the red SPX line will finish the month lower than it began.

Fiscal Policy Outlook

The table below shows the total federal government withdrawals from their accounts at the Federal Reserve Bank. A withdrawal by the federal government is a receipt/credit for the private sector and, therefore, a positive for asset markets.

Federal outlays have increased relative to the previous month, returning to over $3 trillion annually. Positive rates of change and acceleration in spending have historically aligned with stronger equity performance. However, year-over-year federal deficits are declining, implying a shrinking private sector surplus and a less favorable environment for risk assets.

At the larger macro level, and now that the Federal fiscal year is coming to an end, we are able to compare spending year over year. The chart below from CBO shows that federal deficits year over year are in decline, and the implication of this is that the private sector surplus is also in decline year over year, which is not good for risk asset markets.

Fiscal milestones coming up: There was a large federal tax collection in the middle of September, and this is what gave us the large and damaging private sector deficit/federal government surplus budget result reported above. The bad news is that there is another large federal tax collection in the middle of October. The only good news is the small treasury interest payment at the beginning of both October and November.

The federal government, though not suffering from a debt limit, does not have spending authority in the form of an approved budget or continuing resolution. Congress' inability to pass an approved budget or continuing resolution has caused a government shutdown. A government shutdown turns off the main liquidity spigot and could very quickly cause a risk market retrace if left unresolved.

A government shutdown adds further uncertainty and could exacerbate downside moves.

Monetary Policy Outlook

At its September 2025 meeting, the Federal Reserve lowered the target range for the federal funds rate to 4.00%–4.25%. This coordinated global move is expected to result in looser monetary conditions worldwide, lowering the cost of credit and helping to ease inflation, which had been held up by high interest rates.

Fed Rate Stance

Last month I speculated that if a rate-lowering regime was to start, it would start after the Jackson Hole central bankers meeting. This was because it would give the central bankers a chance to get together and plan a mutual way forward on rate changes in lockstep so that the impact on foreign exchange rate price parity would be measured, as this has a huge impact on capital flows.

This has happened, and the decision to lower has been made, and we can expect a general lowering of interest rates worldwide, and this will bring about a general lowering of the price of credit and be deflationary. Price inflation had reached a point where it had more of less coalesced with the price of credit, and it was interest rates that were keeping it high. Lowering rates now will also lower inflation.

This situation reminds me of one of my favorite quotes from Mr. Robert P. Balan with regard to US foreign exchange [FX] parity and how it leads the world in fiscal and monetary policy.

The USD is the world reserve currency. Central banks across the world aim to maintain their FX parity with the USD. When the USA creates more USD is allows the rest of the world to also create more currency so that FX parity is maintained and vice versa. As fiscal and monetary stimulus in the U.S. are unleashed, and the rest of the world follows. In other words, if the U.S. creates a lot of dollars, it is effectively lending its exorbitant privilege to the rest of the world —- as long as capital is allowed to flow freely. The main difference between the first meme and this one lies in the extent to which the rest of the world accepts the invitation.

We believe that, if given the chance, the rest of the world will eagerly jump the gun in this regard, in effect printing their own currency in direct proportion to the pace with which the U.S. does it.

(Source: Mr Robert P Balan, Predictive Analytic Models.)

The following chart plots key indicators used to estimate what the Fed might do next and has proven very predictive.

Market Signals

The federal funds rate [FFR] is led by a multitude of co-factors, and these are listed and discussed below.

1. The orange SPX line has broken out into all-time new highs.

2. The green CPI line has ticked up but is still following an overall descending trajectory.

3. The pink change rate of the 10-year bond yield has risen but generally describes a falling trajectory. The purple nominal 10-year bond rate has fallen and looks to be making a flag pattern, indicating it is building for a major move either up or down. Given the change rate pattern is generally descending, the major move for yields is likely downward.

4. The dark green growth rate of GDP ticked up.

5. The brown unemployment rate has ticked up (shown inverted), meaning less unemployment short term. Generally this line is in a shallow falling trajectory toward more unemployment.

6. The black non farm payrolls rate of change is in a shallow descent.

Key indicators suggest further rate cuts are likely:

  • The S&P 500 has reached new highs.
  • Inflation (CPI) remains on a downward trajectory.
  • Yields on 10-year Treasuries are declining, indicating falling growth expectations.
  • Unemployment is rising, and payroll growth is slowing.

All these factors support a bias toward looser policy in the coming months.

Real Estate Cycle Analysis

Another factor the Fed needs to take into consideration is the condition of the real estate market, as this underpins the bulk of the US capital structure, being about 70% home mortgages.

The U.S. housing index has declined for six consecutive months, raising the risk of a financial crisis if property values fall further. Homebuilder stocks, such as Home Depot and the SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF, have failed to reach new highs, signaling a likely end to the real estate cycle, which experts forecast to peak in mid-2026.

Last month I explained how one of the key leading factors to follow with regard to the real estate cycle is the performance of the homebuilder stocks such as Home Depot (HD) and the SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB).

The chart below shows that it looks like homebuilder stocks have put in their lower second top and are on the way down again. Only a much larger rate cut could have reversed this outcome. Rates did come down, but it was too little, too late.

From a first principles point of view, no amount of rate cuts or fiscal stimulus was going to cause the real estate cycle not to terminate at the forecast time. Demographic trends are also unfavorable, with key home-buying cohorts peaking and set to decline, limiting the potential for a housing market rebound regardless of policy moves.

Global Macro Trends

At the world macroeconomic level, we have the G5 chart below. The chart shows the spending level of the top five world governments (the G5) in a change rate format together with a range of risk assets such as gold (GLD), US Dollar (DXY), and the stock market (SPX).

The green broken circle line shows how the liquidity factors are pointing to a year-end rally where risk asset prices will tend to nest.

The circle with the red broken line shows how liquidity factors are pointing to a major downward movement in 2027, and this is the direction that risk asset prices are likely to nest when that time comes.

In order to be able to show the gold price on the same chart at the same scale, the gold price will have to be inverted to catch its continued move up as liquidity factors worsen going into the big drop in 2027.

Global liquidity tide remains high enough to lift risk assets into the 2026 real estate peak—before an inevitable ebb begins in 2027.

Conclusion & Investment Outlook

The national fiscal flows are weak month over month against a weak global background, and calls for caution until the last quarter of this year, when the brown G5 spending line above starts to rise again into 2026.

The government shutdown is an unwelcome and unnecessary asset price risk that comes at a time of fiscal flow weakness, making any downside risk asset movement larger than it would otherwise be. Given the underlying macro fiscal flow background, any market dip that occurs could present a late-cycle opportunity to buy and make a nice trading profit but should be made with careful downside risk protection.

The 20-year seasonal averages shown in the chart above point to flat markets during the rest of the summer and a risk asset market rally at the end of the year, and this is the most likely outcome. The government shutdown must be resolved to the upside if the standard seasonal pattern is to repeat and give us a year-end risk asset market rally.

data.title: JABS: Investment Grade ABS Securities Via This Fund
data.description:
Thesis

We have covered a number of funds from Janus Henderson in the past, and today we are doing a piece on the latest addition to the cohort of fixed-income ETFs offered by the platform. Our article will delve into the Janus Henderson Asset-Backed Securities ETF (NYSEARCA:JABS), an ETF launched in July 2025 by the asset manager.

What does JABS do?

Let us start by looking at what JABS aims to do as per its objective:

Offers direct access to the growing and complex asset-backed securities market often underrepresented in fixed-income benchmarks. For investors looking for sector diversification and higher yield potential, JABS aims to generate income by actively allocating to high-quality opportunities across the vast U.S. consumer lending market.

JABS is a fund designed to purchase investment grade securitizations, and thus provide a higher yield than T-Bills but with contained risk metrics. The fund's portfolio is a reflection of its objective, via its investment grade composition:

  • AAA: 14.5%
  • AA: 10%
  • A: 74%
  • BBB: 1.5%
  • Other: residual

Please note the ETF is overweight single-A rated assets, and has virtually no sub investment grade exposure. From this angle the fund is limiting any potential downside from credit risk.

We recently provided an update on its sister fund, Janus Henderson Securitized Income ETF (JSI), a name which has suffered a -1.5% drawdown because of its holdings in the Tricolor securitizations. The holdings that caused most of the damage were below investment grade. In the ABS world, sub-investment-grade names can attach and default if the underlying pool of loans is distressed. The probabilities for investment grade paper are very small:

The above are the issue-weighted cumulative impairment rates for North American ABS bonds. We can see that on a 1-year time horizon, single-A securities only have a 0.06% impairment rate, versus 5.9% for CCC ones.

Holdings characteristics

Now that we have established what JABS aims to do, and we saw its ratings parsing, let us look a bit deeper into its composition.

From a sectoral standpoint, the ETF has the following allocations:

  • ABS: 88%
  • CLO: 5%
  • CMBS: 2%
  • Equity: 5%

As per the parsing, the ETF is overweight ABS securities via its composition. The name currently holds 90 securities, with a weighted average maturity of 3.2 years and an effective duration of 2 years. The fund, therefore, is on the shorter duration side, but not a cash parking vehicle since its duration is significantly above 12 months. The fund does not provide a more granular parsing into the ABS category sub-sectors, but an investor can download the full portfolio here.

Some of its yield characteristics and other analytics are:

  • Portfolio yield to worst: 5.2%
  • 30-day SEC yield: 5%
  • Expense ratio: 0.33%

The 30-day SEC yield is currently offering a 100 bps spread over Fed Funds, while the entire portfolio yield to worst is not much higher. An investor should therefore frame this fund as a short-duration one where an excess spread over Fed Funds can be obtained via investment-grade ABS securitizations.

Performance and competitors

Since the name was just issued, we do not have much in terms of performance data, but we can look at the fund versus a similar name:

We have covered the Angel Oak Ultra-Short Income ETF (UYLD) in the past with a 'Buy' rating, and UYLD has a similar mandate and composition. UYLD has over $1 billion in assets and has performed admirably since its inception, while its expense ratio is similar at 0.34%. We can see from the above table the two funds have a similar total return so far.

We view JABS as the Janus Henderson competitor to the very successful UYLD, but its performance and composition matter significantly. We saw the Janus managers make a misstep via the Tricolor securitizations in JSI, and a large position in a bad securitization can make or break JABS as well.

Just as a reference point, let us look at how investment-grade ABS bonds have done versus other similar market instruments:

Since January 2023, the clear winner is AAA CLOs via the Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA), but UYLD comes in second with a total return of 19.23%. UYLD beat simple T-Bills funds like SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL) or even higher duration names like the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active ETF (MINT).

The graph above shows us there is good value in the liquidity premium commanded by short-duration ABS bonds, and a good portfolio selection can result in good long term results. From an asset class perspective, therefore, JABS is set up for success as long as the portfolio managers do a good job at credit selection.

Not all is well in ABS securitizations

While the forward looks bright, one needs to understand that the portfolio managers need to do a good job for that to happen. When looking at the market, not all is well when it comes to ABS bonds:

When looking at the most recent Carvana (CVNA) securitizations, we can observe a very high level of cumulative losses versus prior vintages, losses that are tracking towards 30%, all while rating agencies assumed 14% to 16% in their projections. What does this mean? It means subordinate and mezzanine tranches in Carvana securitizations might incur actual losses, and even senior tranches are trading at a discount given the poor overall performance in the underlying loans.

If a fund had bought any portion of the capital structure in the Carvana securitizations at issuance, it might now be significantly under the purchase price. Security selection therefore matters.

Conclusion

JABS is a fixed-income exchange traded fund. The name is aimed at generating income by focusing on short-dated investment-grade ABS bonds. The ETF comes with a 5% 30-day SEC yield and a 0.33% expense ratio. The name is a direct competitor to UYLD, which we cover, and is a very successful fund. As long as the portfolio managers do a good job at selecting credits from good issuers, JABS is set for success.

data.title: Mister Car Wash: Too Cheap At 9x Free Cash Flow
data.description:
Shares of Mister Car Wash (NASDAQ:MCW) have been battered, declining 35% year-to-date, significantly underperforming the Russell 2000 index (IWM) which has increased 7.5% over the same period. While Mister Car Wash did make a slight downward revision to the midpoint of its full-year guidance, broadly speaking, the business is stable and growing. While shares have declined 68% from its IPO price, the business has steadily grown sales and EBITDA since coming public in 2021. While shares traded for nearly 30x EBITDA shortly after coming public, today's valuation is much more reasonable at just 7x 2026 estimated EBITDA. Beyond a discounted valuation, there are other reasons to like Mister Car Wash as a long-term investment, notably:

  • As the second largest car wash operator in the US, Mister Car Wash has a top 3 market share in nearly all of the local markets where it competes.
  • Nearly 3/4 of Mister Car Wash's revenue comes from monthly car wash 'subscribers' - this revenue is stickier than ad hoc retail washes. More importantly, Mister Car Wash's subscription model and high local market share help it to fend off competitors in a fiercely competitive industry.
  • The company has a strong balance sheet with net debt to EBITDA below 2.6x.

At today's price of $4.72, I estimate that Mister Car Wash trades at just 9x 2026 free cash flow (excluding growth capital expenditures). For a stable business with a leading market share and some competitive advantages, this seems overly discounted.

The combination of a reasonably attractive business and a strong balance sheet coupled with a beaten-up valuation makes Mister Car Wash a 'Buy' in my view. Accordingly, I have purchased a position in the stock.

Overview & Business Model

With over 500 locations, Mister Car Wash is the second largest car wash operator in the US, behind only Whistle Express (which surpassed Mister Car Wash earlier this year after acquiring Take 5 from Driven Brands). Mister Car Wash has grown rapidly, with its unit count more than doubling over the past eight years. This has been achieved primarily organically through opening new car washes. Unlike many car wash operators, which rely on one-off car washes, Mister Car Wash derives 75% of its revenue from it's 2.2 million UWC (Unlimited Wash Club) members. UWC is a monthly subscription whereby people pay a monthly fee for unlimited car washes.

Beyond having a more stable revenue base provided by subscriptions, having a relatively dense local car wash network coupled with a base of subscribers creates some barrier to entry for potential new competitors. All else equal, a potential competitor would likely do better in a market without a branded, well-funded competitor that has a dense network (subscribers can get their cars washed at any Mister Car Wash location) with a large subscriber base who are already paying a monthly fee for car washes (and are less likely to give new entrants a try).

Industry Competitive Dynamics

While I see Mister Car Wash as being well positioned (as described above) the car wash business has become more competitive over the past couple of years. 2021-2023 saw a greater influx of new entrants/location openings by existing players. The impetus for opening more new locations was to some extent driven by low financing costs during the zero interest rate period coupled with the attractive economics on new builds (low financing costs also resulted in overly generous terms from net lease real estate partners). The good news is that the rate of new openings has slowed, with some operators exiting the business altogether. As mentioned earlier, Driven Brands (DRVN), which had been aggressively opening car washes, generated poor returns and halted new openings before ultimately deciding to exit the business by selling to Whistle.

Similarly Zips Carwash, which went on a debt-fueled expansion to 260 locations, filed for bankruptcy earlier this year and shed some locations as part of its restructuring. While the industry will remain competitive, the number of new openings has declined (discussed recently by management as shown below), and I expect to see a more measured pace of new openings going forward.

While Mister Car Wash experienced otherworldly annual same-store sales growth of 9% from 2010-2019, the influx of new competition has impacted results. Same-store growth slowed to 5% in 2022, 0.3% in 2023, 3% in 2024, and 3.6% in 1H25 (I excluded 2020-21 because of the pandemic impact). Over this period EBITDA increased from $282 million in 2022 to $321 million in 2024 (and management guides to $338-342 million for 2025). While this is a marked slowdown from the blistering pace of 2010-2019, overall I see it as a creditable performance given the increase in competition.

Valuation

With its share price having declined 70% from its IPO price while the business has experienced continued growth in EBITDA and free cash flow, valuation multiples have compressed meaningfully. While it was irrational that Mister Car Wash once traded at 30x EV/EBITDA, I see today's valuation (shown above) of 7x EBITDA, 8.6x EBITDAX (EBITDAX = EBITDA less maintenance capital expenditures), and 9x free cash flow as being too cheap.

With a leading market position, recognized brand and subscriber base, and strong balance sheet, I think a more reasonable valuation for the business is 13-15x free cash flow. A 13-15x multiple is consistent with a stable business producing a normalized 2-3% same-store sales/free cash flow growth rate. This implies a fair value of $7-8 per share (45-65% upside).

Risks

  • Car washes are a discretionary purchase, and a recession could hurt demand and negatively impact revenue, EBITDA, and free cash flow.
  • While the rate of new openings has subsided as many new locations failed to produce expected results, there is a risk that competitors open more new locations, which would negatively impact Mister Car Wash. That said, while same-store sales and EBITDA growth slowed due to competition, I view the company's relatively strong performance as validating the competitive advantages of its subscription business model.
  • Private equity firm Leonard Green owns 65% of the shares. Should they decide to exit their position, this could put significant downward pressure on the share price. Alternatively, there is a risk that Leonard Green could seek to buy the 35% of the company it doesn't own at a price below my $7-8 per share fair value estimate (takeunder risk).

Conclusion

Despite an influx of new competitors, I am impressed with Mister Car Wash's ability to continue to grow same-store sales, EBITDA, and free cash flow. I believe the market has overly discounted the business, and as such, have purchased a position in the stock.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: BDC Valuations Have Blown Past Fed Rate Expectations
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund [CEF] market activity from both the bottom-up – highlighting individual fund news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the first week of October. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company [BDC] as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

BDCs remained under pressure, falling around 1% on average for the week. Underperformers included nearly all the higher-valuation BDCs such as HTGC, GLAD, TSLX, TRIN, and MAIN. This dynamic is not unusual in a period of lower prices. That said, the right valuation tail, as measured by the difference between the average and median valuations, remains very extended. If prices continue to fall, we would expect higher-valuation BDCs to underperform, as was the case in previous down markets such as during the Energy crash and 2022.

The median valuation has recently briefly moved below its Liberation Day trough, despite still very high net income levels and a strong economy as indicated by a 3.8% Atlanta Fed GDPNow estimate.

The twelve-month trailing yield has only been higher during recessions. Even if we adjust this by where the Fed is expected to take the policy rate, the sector yield will remain at unusually high levels, something that is not the case elsewhere in the broader income market. What many investors seem to be missing is that, in many cases, the past price movement is more important to the yield of floating-rate assets than the future direction of short-term rates.

This is certainly the case today for the BDC sector, where the price has overshot to the downside the future drop in the sector's net income, as we discuss below.

Market Themes

One of the reasons many investors have for avoiding BDCs is that the likely future drop in short-term rates will cut net investment income across the sector.

The valuation derating of the sector in the last couple of months added around 2.25% to the NII price yield of the median BDC. This works out to around 10 rate cuts given the NII yield sensitivity of each cut. Mind you, the market expects only 5 rate cuts until the end of the Fed rate cycle. This is the same that the Fed expects as well.

In short, the drop in valuations has increased yields by more than double of what the Fed rate cuts are expected to take away. We can argue that the rest comes from the market expecting losses; however, this isn’t correct, as these losses are not priced in anywhere else - either in credit CEFs or in credit spreads.

For today’s BDC valuations to make sense, you’d have to argue that BDCs will deliver outsized losses relative to other credit assets; i.e. the historic picture of lower BDC losses relative to high-yield corporate bonds would have to be reversed.

Sure, there is increased demand for private credit as a hot asset class, and this could lead to some marginal lending, generating losses. However, this has been a risk for some time now and there is no evidence it is actually happening, particularly at the higher-quality end of the sector. We expect Q3 returns in the sector to come in pretty strong, which should provide a boost to prices.

Market Commentary

The First Brands bankruptcy continues to reverberate across the market. As far as exposure in the direct lending sector, it appears that the combination of first and second lien debt amounts to a bit over $200mn. This is spread across 10 private and 4 public BDCs

On the public side they are PSEC, GECC, PSBD and PFX. This is not a massive surprise. As most subscribers know, our BDC coverage skews towards the higher-quality BDCs - there is not much point in covering badly run BDCs, as the basic rule of thumb is that these are not interesting at any price. Out of the 4 public BDCs, only PSEC is covered, and only because it is one of the largest BDCs. PSEC and GECC carry nearly all of the public BDC exposure at $85mm with PSEC marking both first- and second-lien holdings at par - not a great look.

Arguably, the most important question at the moment is whether the broad sell-off we are seeing in the sector is due to the overhang of First Brands and Tricolor noise. So far the market does not appear to be pricing this in across the sector - the affected BDCs have not sold off particularly hard relative to the unaffected BDCs. In the absence of any other convincing explanation (borrower stress, falling economic activity, short-term rates, etc.) it’s fair to say that yes - concerns about sector-wide losses are weighing on BDC prices.

If this is correct, then this is a very favorable setup for the sector. This setup happens when, say security A is affected by some poor fundamentals, which causes a drop in sentiment, which then causes a related security B to fall as well despite B not being affected by the same development. At this point, investors appear to be selling first and asking questions later. This is resulting in a very favorable environment for adding BDC exposure, which we are doing.

Stance and Takeaways

This week we topped up our MSDL, NCDL, BXSL, and OTF holdings. We also reduced FDUS, which has shrugged off much of the sector volatility. None of the holdings have First Brands exposure. The new allocations are using capital previously allocated to PFXNZ - a BDC bond. It has performed very well and is trading near a 3.5 year high.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Stalls In October As High Prices And Job Worries Persist
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment was practically unchanged in October, remaining at its lowest level since May. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index inched down 0.1 points (-0.2%) to 55.0 this month. The latest reading was higher than the forecast of 54.1. Consumer sentiment has fallen by 22.0% (15.5 points) compared to a year ago.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regards to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month: a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment moved sideways this month. At 55 index points, sentiment is virtually unchanged from September. Improvements this month in current personal finances and year-ahead business conditions were offset by declines in expectations for future personal finances as well as current buying conditions for durables. Overall, consumers perceive very few changes in the outlook for the economy from last month. Pocketbook issues like high prices and weakening job prospects remain at the forefront of consumers’ minds. At this time, consumers do not expect meaningful improvement in these factors. Meanwhile, interviews reveal little evidence that the ongoing federal government shutdown has moved consumers’ views of the economy thus far.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. The current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 34.7% below its average reading of 84.3 (arithmetic mean) and 33.8% below its geometric mean of 83.1, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 1st percentile of the 574 monthly data points in this series.

To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In October, the CECI inched up to 61.0. This represents a 1.0% increase from the previous month but a 6.0% decline from a year ago. This reading was above the forecast of 61.0.

Meanwhile, the CEI fell for a fourth straight month to 51.2, its lowest level since May. This represents a 1.0% decline from the previous month and a 30.9% drop from one year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 51.7.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations ebbed from 4.7% last month to a still-high 4.6% this month. Long-run inflation expectations held steady at 3.7%. Inflation expectations for both time horizons are about midway between the readings seen a year ago and the highs seen this year in April and May in the wake of the initial announcements of major tariff changes.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy.

However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Vicor Corporation: Great Technology, Execution Trapped In Time
data.description:
Vicor Corporation (NASDAQ:VICR) is a case study in how technical superiority doesn’t always translate to commercial execution. Two decades ahead of its time, Vicor was once the definition of high-efficiency modular power conversion. Its Factorized Power Architecture (FPA) anticipated the current trend from 12-volt to 48-volt power distribution. And yet, Vicor’s bottomline remains lackluster, and unless there’s a miracle somewhere, my calculations show that whatever potential it may have is already baked into the stock price.

Brief Overview of Vicor

Vicor is an Andover, MA-based designer and manufacturer of power conversion components. Founded in 1981 by CEO Dr. Patrizio Vinciarelli, Vicor’s core innovation is the Factorized Power Architecture (FPA), which separates regulation and transformation into two components, reducing power loss and the volume of space it takes on the board.

Vicor’s products include pre-regulator modules (PRMs), voltage transformation modules (VTMs), and newer Converter-housed-in-Package (ChiP) modules that integrate these functions into compact, surface-mountable packages. Vicor’s products have applications in aerospace, defense, data centers and AI, satellites, and automobiles. However, its superior technology has been overtaken by technology that may not be as superior, but is workable, and cheaper.

Vicor’s Patent Enforcement Strategy

Vicor is still making efforts, with novel legal strategies and some technological improvements. The company has adopted an aggressive, interesting strategy to monetize its intellectual property (IP). It is going after rivals infringing on its fixed-ratio bus converters, which are NBMs, or Non-isolated Bus Converter Modules. These NBMs lie at the core of Vicor’s technology. Their job is to take the 48V input and generate a 12V step-down at 97–98% conversion efficiency, with no galvanic isolation. That removal of unnecessary isolation improves usage of critical space, and increases efficiency. Thus, this was critical technology for Vicor’s Factorized Power Architecture (FPA).

Vicor initiated a Section 337 complaint against rivals infringing on its NBM design. This was filed with the United States International Trade Commission, and not the federal or district court system. As this was a Section 337 complaint, the defendants were mainly foreign entities or their US subsidiaries—Delta Electronics Inc. and its various subsidiaries, Hon Hai Precision Industry Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:HNHPF), commonly known as Foxconn, Quanta Computer Inc. (OTC:QUCCF), some other smaller Taiwanese companies, and their US subsidiaries.

Vicor filed the complaint in July 2023, and in February this year, the United States International Trade Commission found in their favor, confirming patent infringement occurred. The resulting order included a limited exclusion order, which limited the import of power modules and unlicensed computing systems from overseas, and a cease and desist order, which prevented the sale of these products in the US.

According to Susman Godfrey, Vicor’s legal counsel, the verdict found that “U.S. Patent Nos. 9,516,761 (“’761 patent”) and 9,166,481 (“’481 patent”) are valid and infringed” in Investigation No. 337-TA-1370. These were not monetary damages, as there could be if this were a federal or district court case; however, these rulings help Vicor protect its IP, and OEMs and hyperscalers who used the infringed products now have to approach Vicor for licensing to continue doing business using these products.

Since the ITC only issues orders but cannot award monetary damages, a parallel lawsuit was also filed in the Eastern District of Texas, and Vicor sought monetary damages from Delta, one of the alleged infringers. The case was dismissed with prejudice, meaning the parties ultimately settled. While no damages were awarded by the court, a settlement meant a monetary gain for Vicor. Notably, licensing rights were not part of this settlement. Delta instead licenses their technology to large end-user companies, the so-called hyperscalers, rather than companies that were importing systems with Vicor’s patent-protected modules.

The extent of these legal actions went beyond the US. As a related development showed, a US district court also barred Foxconn through a preliminary injunction from seeking arbitration against Vicor in China. The arbitration request sought to use the small-print boilerplate language in some of their purchase orders of Vicor products to claim “unlimited royalty-free licenses to Vicor patents.” While the outcome is still unknown, the preliminary injunction is a positive sign in Vicor's favor.

This sort of legal action is especially suitable in the current political dispensation, where there’s a strong defensive posture against real or perceived threats to US economic interests.

Licensing and Monetization Model

Along with these legal actions, Vicor has been aggressively persuading OEM manufacturers to directly license tech from Vicor instead of going through third parties. Thus, in Q4 2022, they signed their first OEM license, and this year, they signed their first hyperscaler license agreement (Google, according to Needham analysts). The company says licensing fees will bring in $200 million through 2026, with potentially another $400 million from additional licensing agreements. For a company that generated $359 million in net revenue in 2024, that is a very big windfall.

The problem with this is that these are one-time payments - or, at least, the company has avoided clarifying whether or how much of this is recurring. For example, here’s the brief exchange we had on the issue, when Richard Shannon with Craig-Hallum questioned management on the recurrent nature - or lack thereof - of the settlement/licensing fees/royalties:

…Is this settlement -- will we see any ongoing royalties from this customer? Or is it fully paid up in any manner? Can you describe who this is either by name or kind of a company, OEM, hyperscaler, et cetera?...

Patrizio Vinciarelli

So I cannot disclose any of the details that you're looking for. I can only say that at the shareholders meeting that there's been no license in connection with this particular action. So you should not assume that the parties involved got a license and by mutual license, they are able to keep doing what may have been subject to exclusion order and potentially other actions were coming.

As you can probably guess from the QA above, the question that was asked was not about the Delta settlement, but about the hyperscaler licensing deal, because the infringer is not a "customer." So we have no clarity on who was the company that signed the $200 million deal, whether is it recurring, and so on.

Competitive Reality

The other, broader problem is that Vicor is not doing too well as a business. Ignoring one-time monies, revenue has been poor - for example, the $359 million they earned last year was an 11.4% drop from the year before that, and 2023 net revenue was just 1.5% above 2022. The key reason? Vicor’s FPA architecture has been overshadowed by multi-phase regulator technologies from the likes of Monolithic Power Systems, Inc. (MPWR), which are fractionally less efficient in terms of power conversion but much less expensive.

The difference is in design philosophy. Companies like MPWR, Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN), and Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI) use these multi-phase regulators that distribute regulation in a step-down manner, whereas FPA does a single voltage step-down right at the point of load. FPA allows much of the board to carry high-voltage, low-current power, reducing copper loss and improving density. The disadvantage is cost—Vicor's discrete modular design and proprietary packaging make it more expensive to produce than integrated silicon VRMs.

A related problem for end-users is that Vicor alone owns the tech for these highly power-dense and efficient NBMs, so very big companies who prefer to have a diverse supply chain might not want to get involved. That is one reason that NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and AMD switched to MPWR’s multi-phase design in their latest GPUs (NVDA’s H100, AMD MI300) despite FPA’s theoretical superiority with 48V. However, there is an emerging global trend of shifting from 12V to 48V because that can drastically reduce resistive (I²R) losses.

Background: Understanding the 12V-to-48V Transition

The 12-volt power distribution standard has been widely used across the electronic and computing systems industry. This was sufficient in most conditions at that time, when power demands per rack were modest. However, many modern applications require much higher power, so the current required at just 12V became extremely high. This causes energy waste through resistive (I²R) losses—energy dissipated as heat when electrical current (I) passes through the resistance (R') of conductors.

These losses grow with the square of current, so even modest increases in current cause disproportionately large efficiency losses. Shifting to 48 volts reduces the current needed for the same power by a factor of four, cutting resistive losses by roughly sixteen times (4²). This higher voltage also allows for thinner, lighter cables and more compact board layouts without compromising safety.

The good thing about 48V is that it still remains below the SELV (Safety Extra-Low Voltage) threshold of 60V. This is the upper safety limit defined by international electrical standards (IEC 60950/62368). This means 48V systems deliver major efficiency and density gains while staying within safe-to-touch voltage levels, making them ideal for data centers, automotive systems, and high-density electronics.

The 48-Volt Tailwind

Like I noted just now, 48V massively reduces I²R losses. The math is simple - current falls by 4x at 48 V, and losses scale with the square of current. Ordinarily, this would be too perfect, and MPWR would be “good enough,” but modern high-performance servers have extreme power demands per rack, which makes 12V systems impractical for wiring. In EV vehicles and other automotive systems, the same scalability benefit applies - 48V can allow for dramatically lighter wire harness:

…delivering 950W of 12V power (79A) across a 12ft. wiring harness requires a 4AWG cable that weighs roughly 2kg (4.1 lbs.). In contrast, powering the same 950W load with 48V can be accomplished using 10AWG wire, which weighs 0.24kg (>0.5 lb.), resulting in an 85% savings in weight (Figure 2).

48V is still well within the SELV threshold (<60 V), so it is a “sweet spot” between efficiency and safety. This has utility in many modern high-power demanding applications. And Vicor holds the IP to it - as those Taiwanese companies just found out.

The favorable legal outcomes have shown that a) what Vicor has is original and the patents are proven to be enforceable, and b) it is widely copied, i.e., there’s a large market for it. That last point has been even further bolstered by the new 48V trend. However, this has not converted to a decided change in the company's operational trajectory or bottomline.

That brings us to the valuation question: should we value this company for what it has done in the last few years, or should we consider what it can do with its reinforced IP moat and technical superiority? Vicor’s new ChiP (Converter-housed-in-Package) technology is FPA-based but is more easily assembled modules with standard SMT lines. Vicor’s trademark is its power density. But Vicor now needs mass manufacturability and cost parity with integrated multi-phase solutions. Can ChiP make that happen, giving Vicor an edge over larger rivals like Monolithic and Texas Instruments?

Valuation and Peer Comparison

Vicor’s current market cap is ~$2.3 billion. The stock has been volatile this year, going up and down—and the valuation seems conservative, with a price to sales ratio near 6X. Relatively speaking, however, TXN and ADI trade at 10-11X, while MPWR is currently at ~18X—but these companies have diversified portfolios or high profitability.
