MPWR, especially, has seen noteworthy numbers, growing an impressive 121% (EPS') in 2024, as it got design wins from Nvidia. It generated $2.21 billion in revenues last year, with 21% YoY growth.

Now, let us look at intrinsic valuation using two models: one, where the one-time IP/licensing money kicks in, but Vicor continues as before, and two, where Vicor takes strong advantage of the 48V trend and becomes an operational success.

Here are my inputs: Vicor had revenues of $359.1 million in 2024. Their Q2’25 cash & equivalents were $338.5 million. There was no financial debt on the balance sheet (only leases). Their shares outstanding were 45 million, with 33.14 million in common shares and 11.74 million in Class B shares. They booked the $45 million settlement in Q2, while management guided to ~$200 million “returns/licensing-related revenue” through ~Mar’26.

For both scenarios, I will assume core DCF settings as: WACC 11%, terminal growth 3%, tax 21%, D&A 5% of sales, CapEx 6–7% of sales, and change in Net Working Capital (ΔNWC) 1–2% of revenue growth. ΔNWC will be higher in the second scenario.

Now, for Scenario A, which has an IP focus, but flat growth otherwise, we will calculate for the period between 2025 and 2030. We reduce base/product/royalty revenue growth by 1% every time, so in 2025 we have $390 million, which grows 5% in 26-27, 4% in 28-29, and 3% in 2030. Operating margins on base revenue grow from 10% to 15% by 2030. Fees from IP are assumed to be $120 million in 2025, $80 million in 2026 (from management statement of ~$200 million during this period). This tapers down to $20 million per year in recurring royalties between 2027 and 2030.

Applying these revenue and margin assumptions to cash generation after tax gives me these free cash flow (FCF') outcomes (2025→2030):≈ $112 million, $88 million, $50 million, $56 million, $61 million, $67 million (each year 2025→2030). These numbers have been rounded up to account for small percentage differences in my inputs.

Now I'll do the DCF valuation. I get $318 million as the PV of explicit FCFs between 2025 and 2030. PV of terminal value is $458 million. That gives me an Enterprise value of $776 million. Given net cash of $339 million, I get an equity value of $1.15 billion. Dividing across ~45 million shares, I get a per share value of ~$24.78 in the first scenario.

Moving on to Scenario B, where I calculate an incremental value add through quality manufacturing execution between 2025 and 2030, we start with Scenario A, where we get an Equity value of $1.15 billion (~$25/share rounded). What we are essentially assuming is that Vicor retains IP revenue through scenario A, but strong execution and ChiP manufacturing incrementally add to it, does not replace it. Here we assume a higher net revenue growth, so starting at 2025 base revenue of $390 million, we get 20% growth in 2026, 18% in 2027, 15% in 2028, 12% in 2029, and 10% in 2030. That is $780 million in 2030.

We ramp up operating margin by 7 pts over Scenario A, so we have 12% at base ending at 22% in 2030. We front-loaded capex to 7% and 6% of sales, and ΔNWC we set at ~2% to reflect inventory and receivables.

From these assumptions, we get additional FCFs of $43 million, $54 million, $71 million, $92 million, $109 million, and $127 million during the years calculated. The first couple of years are slightly optimistic, but that averages out in the latter years. Combining these numbers with Scenario A, we get combined FCFs of $155 million, $142 million, $121 million, $148 million, $170 million, and $194 million.

Now, I calculate the DCF using 11% WACC and a 3% terminal growth rate. I get PV of explicit FCFs: $647 million (= $318 million A + $329 million B). PV of terminal value is $1.33 billion (= $458 million A + $872 million B). I get an enterprise value of $1.98 billion. Adding net cash, I get an equity value of $2.32 billion. That gives me a per share price of $54.

Vicor’s current share price is $49, so most of this is baked into the share price. Unless execution is near perfect, I would consider VICR to be trading at a premium.

Conclusion and Outlook

Vicor surely has great technology, as its patent win and licensing deals demonstrate. However, technology alone is not enough here, and larger rivals have gone way beyond Vicor’s reach with their inexpensive, “good enough” products that are scalable and adequately efficient. Vicor may well execute flawlessly on both licensing and ChiP, but it is not going to be able to capture meaningful share in the AI and data center markets, where having a diverse supply chain and cost per watt advantages outweigh technical superiority.

At $49 per share, much of Vicor’s future potential is baked into the stock. An investor buying it now will be paying a premium, unless Vicor executes flawlessly and consistently until the end of the decade, in which case there will be a small percentage of appreciation.
